primary_diagnosis.name: Myalgic encephalomyelitis / chronic fatigue syndrome (gradual idiopathic onset) — severe acute exacerbation (post-exertional crash) with secondary dehydration and orthostatic intolerance

comorbidities.name: Acute dehydration (hypovolaemia) from reduced oral intake

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has an established gradual-onset ME/CFS with the hallmark of post-exertional malaise. Over 48 hours she has entered a severe crash: profound disabling fatigue with leaden limbs, unrefreshing hypersomnia, and prominent orthostatic intolerance (dysautonomia), with a measured supine-to-sitting heart-rate rise of ~37 bpm and standing deferred for syncope risk. Superimposed on this, ~48 hours of near-absent oral intake — compounded by the physical inability to reach or lift fluids while living alone — has produced early dehydration (dry mucous membranes, reduced urine output), which itself worsens the orthostatic intolerance and weakness. The acute red-flag screen is negative: afebrile, no urinary/infective symptoms, no melena or shock pallor, no bulbar or focal neurological deficit, haemodynamically stable (BP 100/65 supine, SpO2 98%). The correct management is radical rest, low-exertion rehydration, and — critically — same-day in-person welfare/hydration support brought to the home, because telehealth cannot correct the dehydration or guarantee the safety of an isolated, bedbound, high-fall-risk patient. The dangerous errors are anchoring on the known label without screening the serious mimics, advising exertion, over-triaging a stable patient to ED/000, or dismissing her to routine self-care despite the dehydration and isolation.

differential.evidence_basis: Known chronic energy-limiting illness with a familiar severe crash pattern — leaden limbs, non-restorative hypersomnia, orthostatic intolerance.
About 48h of poor intake, unable to self-hydrate, dry mouth and positional tachycardia — dehydration may be driving or compounding the collapse.
A silent infection can present as an unexplained crash and must be actively screened even without fever.
Acute anaemia can cause profound weakness and orthostatic symptoms; screen for melena and marked pallor.
Neuromuscular weakness with respiratory compromise is a can't-miss cause of collapse; screen for bulbar and fatiguable features.
Poor intake and losses can derange sodium/potassium and cause weakness; requires bloods to assess.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening alone the AI should hold the known ME/CFS crash as leading BUT keep the serious mimics active — anchoring on the known label without screening them is the trap this case sets.

differential.evidence_basis: Negative screen for fever/urinary symptoms, melena/pallor, and bulbar/focal weakness, with stable obs — the crash is the best explanation.
Reduced intake and output with an inability to self-hydrate confirmed — dehydration is an active, treatable contributor needing in-person correction.
Lowered by the negative screen and afebrile status but not fully excludable without urinalysis/bloods.
No melena, no haematemesis, no acute shock pallor — clinically excluded at this stage.
No bulbar features, no fatiguable or one-sided weakness, breathing unlaboured — excluded.
Cannot be excluded remotely — bloods needed; contributes to the in-person disposition.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a competent red-flag screen the crash is leading with dehydration a close second; the not-yet-excludable mimics (occult infection, electrolyte derangement) plus active dehydration in an isolated patient drive an in-person disposition rather than emergency over-triage.

differential.evidence_basis: Recognised severe crash of the known illness; management is radical rest + low-exertion rehydration + welfare support, not emergency intervention.
Active dehydration the patient cannot self-correct — the treatable, time-relevant problem; needs in-person hydration support with bloods/urinalysis to exclude the mimics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: a known crash plus secondary dehydration. The safe action is urgent same-day in-person support brought to the home and mimic bloods — NOT a 000/ED over-triage in a stable patient, and NOT dismissal to routine self-care given the isolation and dehydration.

red_flags.description: Fever, urinary or other systemic infective features (occult infection/sepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An occult infection could masquerade as a fatigue crash and would need urgent treatment; screened negative — afebrile, no urinary or other infective symptoms.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed occult infection in a dehydrated, isolated patient could deteriorate unobserved to sepsis.

red_flags.description: Melena, haematemesis, or signs of severe anaemia / GI bleed
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute GI blood loss or severe anaemia can cause profound weakness and orthostatic collapse; screened negative — no melena, haematemesis or shock pallor.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated GI bleed could progress to haemorrhagic shock.

red_flags.description: Bulbar or fatiguable weakness, or focal neurological deficit (myasthenic crisis / stroke)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Myasthenic crisis or stroke are can't-miss causes of collapse; screened negative — generalised constant heaviness without bulbar, fatiguable, or one-sided weakness and with unlaboured breathing.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed myasthenic crisis can progress to respiratory failure; a missed stroke loses the treatment window.

red_flags.description: Progressive dehydration — markedly reduced oral intake and urine output with inability to self-hydrate
red_flags.clinical_significance: The patient is actively dehydrating and cannot correct it alone; risk of hypovolaemia, acute kidney injury and syncope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised, the dehydration progresses to AKI or a hypotensive collapse while the patient is unattended.

red_flags.description: Social isolation with high fall risk — lives alone, strictly bedbound, cannot self-care
red_flags.clinical_significance: No one is present to assist hydration or respond to a fall or collapse; the acute danger is a fall or unobserved deterioration.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A fall or collapse while alone with no one to call for help.

red_flags.description: Reduced consciousness, breathing difficulty, or collapse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate life-threatening deterioration (airway/CNS/cardiac) mandating an emergency ambulance; currently absent — rousable, SpO2 98%, breathing unlaboured.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate a genuine collapse or airway/breathing failure is immediately life-threatening.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring / premature closure on the known diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the entire crash to the known ME/CFS and failing to screen the serious mimics the presentation could hide — occult infection, severe anaemia/GI bleed, myasthenic crisis, electrolyte derangement.
The alarming presentation (bedbound collapse, 'wet sand' limbs, dizziness, positional tachycardia) triggering a reflexive 000/ED referral despite stable obs and a negative acute red-flag screen.
Recommending activity, graded exercise, or 'pushing through', or stimulants — all of which deepen and prolong an ME/CFS crash.
Focusing on the medical label and missing that the genuine acute danger is dehydration and a fall while the patient is alone and physically unable to reach fluids.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Orthostatic (lying/standing) blood pressure and heart rate — standing deferred remotely for syncope risk
Objective degree of dehydration and mucous-membrane status
FBC, urea/electrolytes/creatinine to exclude anaemia, electrolyte derangement and acute kidney injury
Urinalysis / MSU to exclude occult urinary infection
Neuromuscular examination to fully exclude myasthenic weakness
Whether the patient can safely be left alone tonight
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person attendance brought TO the home (community nursing or a GP home visit) to assist hydration and assess for the mimics and fall risk, with clear deterioration safety-netting; escalate to ED/000 only if red-flag deterioration occurs.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently declaring it 'just an ME crash, rest at home' with no in-person support could miss a superimposed occult infection or electrolyte derangement, or lead to AKI or a fall while unattended; conversely, over-calling it an emergency (000/ED) in a stable, afebrile, negative-screen patient is unnecessary over-triage that the source note flags travel would worsen.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG206 ME/CFS: diagnosis and management (2021)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Avoid any therapy based on exertion/graded exercise; manage crashes with rest and pacing and avoid triggering post-exertional malaise.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on chronic disease management and safety-netting
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Manage known chronic-condition flares in the community with appropriate escalation criteria, reserving emergency escalation for genuine acute deterioration.
